1: The barren woman who never gave birth is called to sing — the reversal of the deepest human shame in the ancient world. The children of the desolate woman will be more than the children of the one who is married. The barren-Zion metaphor follows directly from the Servant's death and vindication (Isaiah 53:10-12): the offspring the Servant sees after his asham-death are the children of barren Zion's abundance. Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 4:27 as the freedom of the heavenly Jerusalem — the mother of those born of promise, not of law.
2: Enlarge the tent — the nomadic image of a family whose expansion requires a bigger dwelling. Zion must prepare for growth she cannot yet see. Do not hold back, lengthen your cords, strengthen your pegs — the structural details of tent-expansion make the command concrete. The faith called for is to act on the promise before seeing the evidence.
3: You will spread out to the right and to the left; your descendants will reclaim nations and resettle desolate cities. The expansion language uses the same vocabulary as the Abrahamic land promises (Genesis 28:14). Zion's multiplication fulfills the patriarchal covenant — another link in the chain from Abraham (Isaiah 51:2) through the Servant to the restored community.
4: Do not be afraid; you will not be put to shame. For you will forget the shame of your youth and remember no more the disgrace of your widowhood. Two past shames: the youth (Egypt, the formative humiliation) and the widowhood (the exile, the loss of the divine husband). Both are swallowed up in the new marriage YHWH announces in v 5.
5: Your Maker is your husband — the two most comprehensive divine titles collapse into the marriage metaphor. YHWH of hosts (creator of the cosmos, commander of its armies), the Holy One of Israel (the utterly transcendent one), the God of the whole earth — all these describe the one who is entering marital covenant with Zion. The shock of the verse is that the incomprehensibly great God is simultaneously the intimate covenant partner of a ruined city.
6: The LORD has called you back like a wife who was abandoned and heartbroken — the exile is reread as marital estrangement, not divorce. The wife of one's youth is the first and most beloved; the call back is a restoration to the original relationship's fullness, not merely a legal reconciliation.
7: For a brief moment I abandoned you, but with great compassion (rahamim) I will bring you back. The ratio is important: exile is a brief moment; return is great compassion. The quantitative imbalance mirrors the Servant's logic (Isaiah 53:4-6): a bounded act of suffering produces unbounded redemptive result. Rahamim (compassion) shares its root with rehem (womb) — this is maternal-gut compassion, the most visceral human tenderness.
8: In a surge of anger I hid my face from you for a moment, but with hesed olam (everlasting covenant love, hesed disp 4, olam disp 3) I will have compassion on you. Hesed is the covenant-loyalty love grounded in the covenant YHWH initiated, not in the worthiness of its object. It is everlasting (olam) — not subject to the fluctuations of anger. The anger was real but momentary; the hesed is constitutive of YHWH's character and therefore permanent. The contrast olam-hesed vs. moment-anger is the theological heart of this passage.
9: The Noah covenant becomes the interpretive framework: just as YHWH swore that the waters of Noah would never again cover the earth — and has kept that oath — so he swears never again to be angry with or rebuke Zion. The Noah oath is the precedent for the Zion oath. Both are unconditional, unilateral divine commitments grounded in YHWH's own character, not in human performance.
10: Though the mountains should move and the hills be shaken, my hesed (covenant love, disp 4) will not move from you, and my covenant of shalom (berit shalom, berit disp 3, shalom disp 2) will not be shaken. Mountains and hills — the most geologically stable features of the ancient world — become the measure of divine commitment: even if they move, YHWH's covenant will not. The covenant of peace bundles two of the highest-disp terms: the binding formal agreement (berit) and the comprehensive wellbeing (shalom) that is both the content and the goal of the covenant.
11: Zion is addressed directly as afflicted city, tossed by storms and without comfort — the same desolate figure as the barren woman of v 1, now about to be rebuilt with precious stones. The gemstone-rebuilding of vv 11-12 is a reversal of the rubble of Isaiah 51:17-20: the same city lying in ruins will be refounded in splendor. The materials (sapphires, rubies, sparkling jewels, precious stones) recall both the gemstones of the high priest's breastplate and the foundation stones of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:19-20).
12: The architectural specifics — towers, gates, walls — all of precious stones. The gate of sparkling jewels is the point of entry into the city; the precious-stone wall is what protects it. Beauty and security are unified in the rebuilt Zion. Revelation 21 draws directly on this imagery for the new Jerusalem.
13: All your children will be taught by the LORD, and great will be their shalom (peace, disp 2). The promised outcome of the covenant of shalom (v 10) is that all Zion's children receive divine instruction directly. This is the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:33-34 — no longer needing to teach neighbor or brother, for all will know YHWH. Jesus quotes this verse in John 6:45 (they will all be taught by God) as the condition of coming to him.
14: You will be established in tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3); you will be far from oppression and will not fear. The community's foundation shifts from precarious human power to YHWH's righteousness — the same tsedaqah that the exiles were called to pursue (Isaiah 51:1) and that YHWH was about to enact (Isaiah 51:5). When YHWH's righteousness is the foundation, terror cannot take root.
15: If anyone attacks you, it is not my doing — YHWH disavows the coming attacks before they happen. The threat from outsiders will ultimately fail: whoever assembles against you will fall before you. The passive falling is YHWH's active judgment; the city needs only stand in its righteousness.
16: YHWH claims credit for creating the blacksmith who forges weapons and the destroyer who wields them. This is the most totalizing sovereignty claim in the passage: the one who makes the weapons and the one who deploys them are both YHWH's creatures. He is not threatened by the weapons made against Zion because he created their makers.
17: No weapon formed against you will succeed, and every tongue that accuses you in court you will refute. The legal and military threats are both covered. This is the inheritance (nahalah) of the servants of YHWH, and their tsedaqah (righteousness, disp 3) is from me. The closing verse reframes the entire chapter: safety, vindication, and righteousness are not Zion's own achievement but her covenant inheritance — received from YHWH, declared by him. Nahalah (inheritance) is the Promised Land vocabulary applied to the eschatological community.
